query: Angular interpolation vs property binding interview question

selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: No single-topic GreatFrontEnd page was confirmed for this exact Angular prompt; the available Angular basics coverage is broader than the question-specific comparison needed here.

competitors.label: Angular Binding Guide
competitors.selectionReason: The official binding guide is the canonical reference for text interpolation, property binding, attribute binding, and related template syntax.
competitors.winReason: Angular.dev is authoritative on binding syntax, but our page now answers the interview trap more directly by pairing boolean-state bugs, ARIA attributes, and object-input stringification in one compact answer.
theirStrengths: It authoritatively explains interpolation, property binding, attribute binding, and signals in Angular templates.
theirGaps: It is less explicitly framed around the interview trap of boolean-ish attributes and the bugs caused by string coercion.

followUpConfusion: Use [attr.*] only when the target is truly an attribute such as ARIA.

realWorldUseCases: Angular.dev explains the binding surface clearly, so the practical use-case comparison stays close.
actionableExamples: The official guide includes concrete binding examples, but our page now puts boolean state, ARIA attributes, and non-string component inputs in one worked contrast.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about the boolean-attribute trap, object-stringification bugs, and when to switch from [prop] to [attr.*].

realWorldUseCases: Keep one example anchored on runtime element state so the answer does not collapse into a syntax-only comparison.

competitors.label: Telerik Property Binding and Interpolation Guide
competitors.selectionReason: This tutorial is a direct competitor because it focuses on the same interpolation-versus-property-binding comparison.
competitors.winReason: Telerik explains the syntax well, but our page is more interview-ready because it now shows the broken boolean attribute, the correct property-versus-attribute split, and the non-string input follow-up together.
theirStrengths: It is directly focused on the same comparison and shows the template syntax concretely.
theirGaps: It is less explicit about the interview-grade follow-up on boolean attributes and attr-vs-prop boundaries.

followUpConfusion: Use [attr.*] only when the target is truly an attribute such as ARIA.

realWorldUseCases: Telerik stays directly on the same practical distinction, so the use-case framing is close.
actionableExamples: Telerik explains the syntax well, but our page now gives the stronger compact example set across boolean state, ARIA attributes, and object inputs.
followUpConfusion: Our page is now more explicit about when attribute interpolation becomes a bug and why [attr.*] is the right escape hatch for ARIA.

realWorldUseCases: Keep the answer anchored on DOM state and coercion bugs rather than letting it read like a syntax glossary.

competitors.label: Swiftorial Property Binding
competitors.selectionReason: This is a direct tutorial competitor for property binding and related template examples.
competitors.winReason: Swiftorial is clear on property binding basics, but our page is stronger on the runtime bug pattern and the property-versus-attribute follow-up that usually decides these interviews.
theirStrengths: It gives clear property-binding examples and stays tightly focused on the syntax.
theirGaps: It does not compare property binding against interpolation through real boolean-attribute or coercion bugs.

realWorldUseCases: use property binding for element state
followUpConfusion: Use [attr.*] only when the target is truly an attribute such as ARIA.

realWorldUseCases: Swiftorial explains property binding clearly, but our page is stronger on when the distinction prevents real UI bugs.
actionableExamples: Its focused examples stay competitive enough that the pure worked-example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: It is lighter on follow-up nuance about boolean attributes, [attr.*], and object-stringification bugs in runtime element state.

actionableExamples: Keep the boolean-state and non-string-input example pair visible so the runtime difference stays easy to scan.
